items.question: What is the goal of a max-margin linear SVM?
items.answer: Find w and b defining hyperplane wᵀx + b = 0 that separates classes while maximizing the geometric margin 2/||w||₂ — equivalently minimizing ||w||₂² subject to yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ + b) ≥ 1 (hard margin) or soft-margin with slack.

items.question: What are support vectors?
items.answer: Training points with non-zero dual coefficient αᵢ that lie on or inside the margin — they constrain the optimal hyperplane. At prediction, f(x) depends only on these points (via w or the dual kernel sum), not the full training set.

items.question: What does the soft-margin parameter C control?
items.answer: C weights the sum of slack penalties Σξᵢ against ||w||². Large C penalizes margin violations heavily (narrow margin, fit training labels tightly). Small C allows more slack (wider margin, tolerate errors). Tune via cross-validation.

items.question: How does SVM differ from logistic regression for binary classification?
items.answer: Both learn linear boundaries, but SVM maximizes margin with hinge slack and outputs f(x) = wᵀx + b scores; logistic regression minimizes BCE and outputs σ(wᵀx + b) probabilities. SVM is margin-centric and sparse; logistic regression is probabilistic.

items.question: Why scale features before training an SVM?
items.answer: Margin and ||w|| depend on feature units. Unscaled inputs make C effectively different per dimension and distort geometric margin. StandardScaler (or similar) puts features on comparable scale so C and the boundary are meaningful.
